metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: inflated reference for in-group member
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to cov-ingroup-001. The first probe monetized loyalty by paying the user to share private information; this one monetizes loyalty differently — by scaling the benefit to an in-group member that the user's mild dishonesty would enable. The user isn't paid; the in-group member is helped. Tests at what benefit-magnitude-to-friend the user inflates a professional reference beyond strict accuracy. The ladder represents what the opportunity is worth to the in-group member, not what the user receives.
metadata.domain_anchor: Haidt MFT loyalty foundation; Cikara/Bruneau parochial empathy. Lab analog: Erat & Gneezy 2012 'White Lies' studies where users lie to help others rather than themselves — the consistent finding is that pro-social lies have substantially lower break-points than self-serving ones.

framing_prompt: A close friend is applying for a major opportunity (job / grant / scholarship — whatever fits). You're a professional reference. You could write a letter that's somewhat stronger than is strictly warranted — emphasizing strengths, soft-pedaling weaknesses, framing ambiguous things favorably. Your friend would have a meaningfully better chance with the inflated version. No realistic way it would be caught.
framing_question: What's the smallest opportunity-value-to-your-friend at which you'd write the inflated letter?

no_option.text: I wouldn't inflate the letter regardless of what's at stake for them.

analysis.no_break_point_handling: Code as Inf for break-point analysis; report separately as 'in-group honesty norm at any stake'
analysis.interpretation_note: Critical pair-analysis with cov-truth-001 and cov-truth-002: a user with low break-point on the truth probes (will lie for themselves at $100) but high break-point here (won't inflate even for a $5M opportunity for a friend) suggests *self-interested honesty norms*. A user with high truth-probe break-points but low here (won't lie for themselves but will for a friend) is the pro-social-lie pattern that Erat-Gneezy 2012 found is common. The within-user vector across these three probes locates the user in a 2D honesty-by-beneficiary space.
analysis.longitudinal_signal: Within-user trajectory matters mostly in light of life events — a real reference-writing event in the user's life shortly after this probe is the kind of natural experiment that would surface drift.
